original_abstract:
Heinrich Bullinger’s *De testamento* is a profound theological and biblico-historical treatise that explores essential themes such as justification by faith, divine accommodation, and the covenant as the central architectural framework of all Scripture. Within this work, Bullinger meticulously addresses the covenant’s continuity from Adam to the New Testament, the role of Abraham’s seed, and the intersection of the covenant with the Law, the Holy Spirit, and the sacraments. Rather than a narrow study on covenantal mechanics, *De testamento* reveals Bullinger as a biblical theologian profoundly concerned with the ethical response of the elect, challenging believers to live with integrity before the sovereign *El Shaddai*.

A linguistic analysis of Bullinger’s Latin terminology—specifically *foedus*, *testamentum*, and *pactum*—demonstrates that he viewed these terms as largely interchangeable. However, he notably exercised caution with the term *pactum* to prevent his readers from conflating his views with contemporary "pactum theology." Furthermore, Bullinger’s semantic consistency surpasses that of his predecessor, Huldrych Zwingli, suggesting a significant degree of intellectual independence in his formulation of covenantal thought.
humanized_abstract:
De Testamento by Heinrich Bullinger is an extraordinary theological and biblical treatise on a variety of issues. Bullinger engages such important issues as justification by faith, divine accommodation, and the covenant as the skeletal structure of Scripture. Bullinger’s thorough examination of the continuity of the covenant from Adam to the New Testament, as well as the relationship of the covenant to the Law, the Holy Spirit, and the sacraments makes him look far less interested in the mechanics of the covenant than in simply being a biblical theologian who wants to call the elect to live a godly life before the holy God who is El Shaddai.

Bullinger’s use of Latin terms for describing the covenant in Scripture includes foedus, testamentum, and pactum. He frequently employs them without any apparent difference of meaning. The use of the term pactum appears to be so infrequent with Bullinger, so as to preclude any identification of his doctrine with the system of pactum theology which was in wide vogue during his day. Bullinger’s employment of these terms shows a consistency far greater than was manifested by his immediate predecessor, Zwingli, and thus demonstrates Bullinger’s independent contribution to covenant theology.
